title: Sum-Product-Set Networks: Deep Tractable Models for Tree-Structured Graphs
abstract: We introduce Graph-Induced Sum-Product Networks (GSPNs), a new probabilistic framework for graph representation learning that can tractably answer probabilistic queries. Inspired by the computational trees induced by vertices in the context of message-passing neural networks, we build hierarchies of sum-product networks (SPNs) where the parameters of a parent SPN are learnable transformations of the a-posterior mixing probabilities of its children's sum units. Due to weight sharing and the tree-shaped computation graphs of GSPNs, we obtain the efficiency and efficacy of deep graph networks with the additional advantages of a probabilistic model. We show the model's competitiveness on scarce supervision scenarios, under missing data, and for graph classification in comparison to popular neural models. We complement the experiments with qualitative analyses on hyper-parameters and the model's ability to answer probabilistic queries.
human_feedback_list:
0. The requirement of full independence in the distribution of a set unit seems quite stringent and potentially unrealistic, for example, in the mutagenesis example where atoms in a molecule would be considered independent conditional on molecule size, which may not be a valid assumption.
1. On the empirical side, to justify the importance of tractability, it would be useful to test some example queries on the learned SPSNs and their domain-specific interpretation beyond marginalizing leaves where the tree structure is fixed.
2. Details on how SPSNs are learned seem to be missing; specifically, while the structure of the SPSN is fixed, it is unclear how the parameters are learned and whether the cardinality distribution is learned and how it is parameterized.
3. Is there any technical reason why the SPSN architecture cannot be extended to directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) rather than just tree-structured data?
4. The related work section is thorough, but it would be useful to better understand the relationship with relational SPNs, especially since the tested datasets come from a relational schema. Specifically, how does one translate a relational schema to a tree schema as in Figures 4-11, and in what way is relational data a particular form of graph-structured data?
5. The statement of Proposition 2 is unclear and seems more like a definition rather than a result.
6. The manuscript does not fully justify or analyze key components of the approach, specifically (1) providing concrete examples of interesting tractable inference queries when the number of variables is not fixed beyond marginalizing leaves, and (2) empirical or theoretical analysis of the impact of Assumption 1 on expressivity and modeling capacity.
7. The writing of the paper would be greatly improved by adding informal intuitions throughout the text and including a complete toy example of a sum-product-set network (SPSN).
8. The authors do not clarify whether it is possible to obtain a distribution over tree-structured graphs using knowledge compilation to compile the distribution into a logical circuit whose parameters can then be learned, and whether there is an expressivity gap between probabilistic circuits (PCs) with and without set units.
9. The authors only briefly mention that the infinite sum required to evaluate the set unit reduces to a finite sum in practice, which is crucial for their tractability results. This should be made more formal in the paper.
10. The paper does not clearly indicate the scale of the experiments performed, and the experiments appear to be toyish.
11. Could the authors please explain what a schema is in an intuitive way, beyond the formal definition? The term 'schema' is familiar in databases but does not seem to translate directly here.
12. Am I correct in understanding that set units only apply to homogeneous nodes?
13. Assumption 1 states that the cardinality distribution vanishes for sufficiently large m. What exactly does this mean? Also, is it correct that elements of the set are independent given the cardinality, i.e., statistical correlations between elements are not considered? This raises questions about the tractability of SPSNs as laid out in Proposition 1.
14. Could the authors please elaborate on Definition 5? Specifically, what structural constraint is being imposed? The phrase 'follow only a single child of each sum unit' suggests determinism, is this the case?
15. The manuscript lacks a transparent and detailed discussion about the implementation of SPSNs, particularly regarding the convergence behavior, model size, sensitivity to parameter initialization, and hyper-parameter tuning.
16. Section 5 is unclear on how the tractability and exchangeability properties of SPSNs are exploited in the experiments, making it difficult to understand how these theoretical properties translate into practical benefits.
17. The experiments, while showing beneficial results on missing values, do not convincingly demonstrate efficient inference over specific parts of the data graph as claimed in the Conclusion.
18. The manuscript would benefit from comparing its results with recent related works, especially those discussed in Section 4, to better situate the contribution and highlight differences.
19. The writing is unclear regarding the distinction between probabilistic circuits (PCs) and sum-product networks (SPNs), which causes confusion since the title evokes SPNs but the text uses PCs interchangeably without clarification.
20. The manuscript should discuss the differences between SPNs and SPSNs earlier in the paper, as this is a key contribution of the work, rather than only mentioning it in Section 3.
21. The manuscript could better articulate its motivation by connecting the problems presented in the introduction with some of the results, specifically clarifying how SPSNs take advantage of the parent-child ancestry inherent in tree-structured graphs differently than competitive generative models.
22. The paper does not clarify if there are empirical boundaries or assumptions when implementing the recursive algorithm described in 'Building SPSNs', nor how large data graphs with multiple heterogeneous nodes and size constraints are handled.
23. Please expand on the method described in Section 4 about building a set of trees based on a user-specified neighborhood, particularly in relation to the similar graph-based approach from Errica & Niepert (2023).
24. The paper addresses an important problem by extending probabilistic circuits to tree-structured graph data, which is interesting and promising for the community.
25. The presentation and writing of the paper are very good, making the main points and mechanisms of the SPSNs method easy to understand even for newcomers.
26. The investigation of the exchangeability property of SPSNs is thorough and well done.
27. No meaningful weaknesses or technical questions were identified due to the reviewer's limited expertise.
llm_feedback:
0. The paper should explicitly discuss the difference between the pseudo-log-likelihood objective used and a true joint distribution over the entire graph, acknowledging that the product of tree-based conditionals does not necessarily define a consistent joint distribution due to graph cyclicity.
1. The paper should analyze the expressive power of the GSPN architecture in terms of the Weisfeiler-Lehman (WL) isomorphism test, specifically whether the probabilistic constraint on the aggregation function limits its ability to distinguish neighborhood structures compared to unconstrained MLPs in GIN.
2. The current model uses a fixed SPN template for all nodes, but SPNs are known to be more powerful when their structure is learned; exploring heterogeneous SPN templates for different node degrees or roles could improve performance.
3. The paper should clarify whether log-space computations are used for sum-product operations to address potential numerical over/underflow issues, as is standard in SPN literature.
4. The experimental evaluation should include comparisons against structure-aware imputation methods such as GRAPE and GAE-based imputation to demonstrate the advantage of the SPN marginalization approach over impute-then-train methods.
5. An ablation study should be conducted to compare the current aggregation function based on the "Switching Parent" decomposition with standard GNN aggregations like Mean or Max normalized to valid priors, to isolate the contribution of the aggregation design.
6. The paper should quantitatively evaluate uncertainty calibration, for example using Expected Calibration Error, to demonstrate that GSPN knows when it is uncertain and is better calibrated than standard GIN models.
7. Include a scalability analysis showing training time and memory usage versus graph size compared to standard GCN/GIN models to help practitioners understand the computational trade-offs of GSPN.
8. Improve notation consistency by using graph-theoretic notation for indexing, such as $h_{u}^{\ell}$ where $u$ is a neighbor of $v$, instead of the current indexing which may be misinterpreted.
9. Improve the integration of Figures 1 and 2 by either combining them or ensuring that the color-coding of nodes in the graph corresponds clearly to the SPN units, to reduce density and improve intuition.
10. Add a brief explanation of how gradients flow through the shortcut connections described as convex combinations of parameters, to clarify the optimization dynamics compared to standard residual additions.
11. Maintain the terminology distinction between "nodes" for trees and "vertices" for graphs consistently throughout the paper and appendix to avoid confusion.
12. State the average percentage of missing features across the dataset when simulating missing data using a Gamma distribution to provide readers with a sense of task difficulty.

title: On the Role of Edge Dependency in Graph Generative Models
abstract: In this work, we introduce a novel evaluation framework for generative models of graphs, emphasizing the importance of model-generated graph *overlap* (Chanpuriya et al., 2021) to ensure both accuracy and edge-diversity. We delineate a hierarchy of graph generative models categorized into three levels of complexity: edge independent, node independent, and fully dependent models. This hierarchy encapsulates a wide range of prevalent methods. We derive  theoretical bounds on the number of triangles and other short-length cycles producible by each level of the hierarchy, contingent on the model overlap. We provide instances demonstrating the asymptotic optimality of our bounds.  Furthermore, we introduce new generative models for each of the three hierarchical levels, leveraging dense subgraph discovery (Gionis & Tsourakakis, 2015). Our evaluation, conducted on real-world datasets, focuses on assessing the output quality and overlap of our proposed models in comparison to other popular models. Our results indicate that our simple, interpretable models provide competitive baselines to popular generative models. Through this investigation, we aim to propel the advancement of graph generative models by offering a structured framework and robust evaluation metrics, thereby facilitating the development of models capable of generating accurate and edge-diverse graphs.
human_feedback_list:
0. The first sentence of the paper claims to introduce a novel evaluation framework for generative models of graphs, but the paper does not actually present such an evaluation framework.
1. The theoretical propositions in the paper are acceptable, but the generative models and experimental results can be improved.
2. The relationship between the generative models and the theoretical contributions is unclear, and the empirical evaluation of the trade-off between overlap and performance seems like a separate paper.
3. The proposed generative models are trivial and not an important contribution. There is no training algorithm for the parameter p, and hyperparameter search is done by grid search.
4. The models cannot replicate graph characteristics with low overlap, which contradicts the paper's claim that the models should exhibit low overlap. This issue is worse on other networks.
5. The details of the experiments are placed in the appendix rather than the main paper.
6. The overlap metric used in the paper may not be a good measure for diversity of small graphs because it does not consider graph isomorphism.
7. How are maximal cliques selected one by one in Algorithm 1? Is there overlap between two maximal cliques?
8. If the input graph is very sparse with weak connectivity (e.g., most nodes have degree 1 or 2), then the sampled graph G_p may be seriously disconnected. Even after adding a sampled second graph G_r, how does Algorithm 1 guarantee the statistics of the combined graph G_u?
9. Why are the statistics results of GraphVAE missing in Figures 3-6 and 9?
10. How are the small subgraphs of benchmarks in Table 2 generated? Is there a unified approach for this?
11. How large a graph can this method be applied to? Are experiments limited to smaller datasets because verifying results on large graphs is difficult?
12. What are the practical applications of the proposed framework, for example in social networks, financial networks, or drug discovery?
13. The categorization of graph generative models into three types (edge independent, node independent, and fully dependent) does not include many recent generative models. The authors should provide more examples of recent generative models such as GDSS, GraphARM, and DiGress that fit into the proposed categorization.
14. The definitions of the edge independent (EI), node independent (NI), and fully dependent (FD) graph generative model categories are vague and lack formal clarity. Specifically, the definition of the FD model is unclear, especially the phrase 'the generative models allow for any possible distribution A'. A more formal and precise definition is needed.
15. The manuscript lacks a detailed explanation of why using overlap is a strong and appropriate evaluation framework for graph generative models. Although accuracy and diversity are emphasized as key evaluation characteristics, it is unclear why overlap effectively captures these aspects.
16. The manuscript lacks comparison of the proposed overlap evaluation metric to commonly used metrics such as Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) and Validity-Uniqueness-Novetly (V.U.N) metrics, which are popular in graph generative model evaluation.
17. The reviewer questions the motivation for establishing bounds or theoretical limits on the number of triangles and other short-length cycles in graph generative models, seeking clarification on why these bounds and their proofs are necessary.
18. The reviewer finds it difficult to understand the key contribution of the paper, specifically whether the proposed evaluation framework is simply to compare the overlap between generated graphs and test graphs.
19. The reviewer asks whether the triangle-based evaluation method is limited to models that generate many triangles, and whether the proposed method can effectively capture graph generation quality for graphs like grid graphs that have fewer triangles.
20. The meaning of the x-axis in Figure 2 is unclear. Although it is understood that lines following the dotted 'True' line are desirable, the exact interpretation of Figure 2 is not well explained.
21. The related works section includes deep graph generative models such as GraphGAN, GraphRNN, MolGAN, and GraphVAE, which focus on multiple input graphs, making it harder to understand that this work targets the single input graph setting. The paper should clearly explain that it targets the single input graph task and include related works such as GraphAE that address this setting.
22. The reviewer notes that V.U.N metrics are not intended for multiple input graphs; validity and uniqueness are computed only on generated graphs, and novelty is computed relative to training graphs. While novelty may be inappropriate for single input graph settings, validity and uniqueness could still be evaluated. Additional experiments on large graphs where validity can be computed (e.g., SBM data) would strengthen the work.
23. The reviewer is concerned that the cubic nature of the theoretical bounds on triangle counts appears very loose and questions their practical applicability and insightfulness for structuring the generative process. They also request synthetic experiments to verify the bounds' validity and seek clarification on the computational cost of the bounding technique as the number of nodes grows.
24. The reviewer requests more clarity on the methodological complexity of using the bounds to achieve the target structure in sampled graphs, specifically asking for a better description of the process of associating probabilities to graph locations (adjacency matrix, locations, or edge lists). They also note that Algorithm 1 briefly describes the methods but lacks detailed explanation in the main body.
25. The reviewer suggests including a scalability analysis of the proposed models, questioning the computational cost as the number of nodes grows.
26. The reviewer requests more clarity on how to use the theoretical bounds to determine which edges to sample to achieve a target structure, suggesting that a pseudocode could help.
27. The reviewer finds the explanation of the cubic bound insufficiently clear and requests further clarification on the novelty of the contribution, especially regarding tighter bounds in terms of overlap and volume. They illustrate this by discussing Erdős–Rényi graphs, noting that producing community structure requires additional constraints and that a cubic bound on triangle counts is expected.
llm_feedback:
0. The term "Node Independent" (Definition 4) may confuse readers because edge probabilities depend on node embeddings. It would be clearer to explicitly link this term to established concepts such as Latent Space Models or Random Dot Product Graphs (RDPG) to help researchers in those sub-fields find the work.
1. The overlap metric (Definition 1) normalizes by the volume of the distribution, which may reduce intuition in practice when the model's generated graph volume differs significantly from the target graph's volume. This behavior should be discussed.
2. The paper claims some bounds are asymptotically optimal but does not discuss whether these tight instances (e.g., complete graph or active-node model) represent real-world graph structures, especially sparse graphs. This discussion would strengthen the paper.
3. The reliance on maximal clique detection in the proposed baselines (MCEI, MCNI, MCFD) poses a significant scalability bottleneck because listing all maximal cliques is NP-hard.
4. The addition of an Edge Independent (EI) residual graph component ($G_r$) to match degree distributions may dilute the higher-order dependencies of the FD/NI models. A sensitivity analysis on how the edge ratio between $G_p$ and $G_r$ affects the triangle bound would be insightful.
5. Using early stopping to vary overlap for deep models like CELL and GraphVAE may disadvantage these models because they are not evaluated at convergence.
6. The paper focuses on local motifs but does not evaluate global graph properties like community structure or diameter, which are important for graph generation.
7. The dataset evaluation is limited to social and citation networks, which are triangle-rich. It would be valuable to test the hierarchy on biological/chemical graphs or technological networks that have different motif distributions.
8. Figure 1 would be improved by explicitly showing the theoretical limit line for Edge Independent (EI) models to visually demonstrate that real-world networks exceed what EI models can achieve at low overlap.
9. Algorithm 1's pseudocode uses sqrt(p) for MCNI derived from Theorem 3, but lacks a comment linking this to the 'active node' logic in Section 3.3, which would improve readability.
10. Table 1 is a good summary but could be improved by adding columns for 'Cycle Bound' or 'Memory Complexity' to provide a more comprehensive overview of trade-offs.
11. Notation for overlap uses both calligraphic \( \mathcal{A} \) and sample \( A \) inconsistently, which should be standardized.
12. Some citations in the Introduction are truncated or formatted incorrectly, such as "Erdos & R...enyi, 1960". LaTeX compilation for special characters like Erdős should be fixed.

title: EqNIO: Subequivariant Neural Inertial Odometry
abstract: Neural networks are seeing rapid adoption in purely inertial odometry, where accelerometer and gyroscope measurements from commodity inertial measurement units (IMU) are used to regress displacements and associated uncertainties. They can learn informative displacement priors, which can be directly fused with the raw data with off-the-shelf non-linear filters. Nevertheless, these networks do not consider the physical roto-reflective symmetries inherent in IMU data, leading to the need to memorize the same priors for every possible motion direction, which hinders generalization. In this work, we characterize these symmetries and show that the IMU data and the resulting displacement and covariance transform equivariantly, when rotated around the gravity vector and reflected with respect to arbitrary planes parallel to gravity. We design a neural network that respects these symmetries by design through equivariant processing in three steps: First, it estimates an equivariant gravity-aligned frame from equivariant vectors and invariant scalars derived from IMU data, leveraging expressive linear and non-linear layers tailored to commute with the underlying symmetry transformation. We then map the IMU data into this frame, thereby achieving an invariant canonicalization that can be directly used with off-the-shelf inertial odometry networks. Finally, we map these network outputs back into the original frame, thereby obtaining equivariant covariances and displacements. We demonstrate the generality of our framework by applying it to the filter-based approach based on TLIO, and the end-to-end RONIN architecture, and show better performance on the TLIO, Aria, RIDI and OxIOD datasets than existing methods.
human_feedback_list:
0. The abstract describes EqNIO as leveraging 'canonical displacement priors' to generalize across arbitrary IMU orientations, but it lacks a clear technical explanation of how these priors work in practice. The abstract also claims generalization stems from 'canonical gravity-aligned frames' and 'equivariant yaw frames,' but it could benefit from a more precise explanation of these transformations and how they are operationalized in the model.
1. The learnable yaw orientation in canonical frames is a promising feature but lacks clarity on how it resolves yaw drift or improves orientation estimation, especially since yaw is typically the most challenging to estimate accurately in inertial odometry due to the absence of an absolute reference.
2. The introduction highlights EqNIO’s generalization and robustness but does not discuss potential limitations or scenarios where the approach may struggle, such as handling different sampling rates, extreme motions where IMU biases may not be fully mitigated, or contexts with poor gravity alignment.
3. While EqNIO is compared to existing neural odometry methods like TLIO and RONIN, the introduction does not delve into specific weaknesses in these prior approaches and how EqNIO addresses these limitations.
4. The paper covers a broad range of related works but may omit some recent or seminal papers in learning-based inertial odometry and equivariant neural networks. The literature review should be comprehensive, including all relevant and recent works to properly position EqNIO within the current research landscape. Given the close relationship between odometry and inertial attitude estimation, the authors are encouraged to explore learning-based approaches to inertial attitude estimation as well.
5. The descriptions of related methods such as TLIO and RONIN are somewhat high-level and lack technical depth, which may not adequately highlight the nuances that differentiate EqNIO from these methods.
6. It is unclear whether ICLR is the most appropriate venue for this research, given that the learning components in the paper are limited mainly to a regression module.
7. The paper assumes diagonal covariance in uncertainty modeling, but the validity of this assumption is only tested by final performance. An in-depth analysis using established uncertainty quantification evaluation tools could improve the paper, even if it is not the core focus.
8. For sensor fusion in visual-inertial odometry, having good uncertainty estimates from the inertial odometry module would be helpful. These estimates should consider modeling errors of neural networks and propagate uncertainties to the final module. Priors can also be defined using physical system properties. Would Bayesian modeling tools be considered for this?
9. The primary weakness of the paper is the clarity of its writing, particularly in understanding the major differences between this work and the previous work RIO: Rotation-equivariance Supervised Learning of Robust Inertial Odometry.
10. A more detailed explanation of the roto-reflection group and its importance is needed to help readers understand this concept and its relevance to the work.
11. A more detailed explanation of the PCA (handcrafted equivariant frame) concept and its relevance is needed to clarify its role and limitations in the method.
12. The figure intended to convey the core idea of the work does not clearly distinguish the differences between this approach and RIO; elaborating on this distinction would strengthen the presentation.
13. Figure captions should specify which data is seen and which is unseen to enhance reader understanding, as method performance highly depends on the amount of seen or trained data.
14. The supplementary material provides better clarity than the main paper; including some of this contextual information directly in the main paper would make it easier for reviewers and readers to follow the method.
15. The general canonicalization scheme used in the paper has been previously proposed by Kaba, Sékou-Oumar, et al. in 'Equivariance with learned canonicalization functions' (ICML 2023), and therefore should not be presented as a novel contribution. The original work must be cited.
16. Although the proposed method reduces drift compared to baselines, the remaining drift is still significant (greater than 2 meters), suggesting that the main problem in inertial odometry is not exact equivariance but likely sensor noise.
17. Why was the canonical equivariance scheme chosen over other equivariance approaches such as frame averaging (Puny et al., ICLR 2022), which also allow adapting existing non-equivariant architectures?
18. There is confusion about the choice and definition of metrics used in the TLIO experiments. Specifically, the definition of MSE in Appendix A.5 seems to be missing the squared norm, making it unclear how MSE relates to the square root of ATE. Also, it is suggested that ATE, RTE, and AYE metrics would be sufficient. Additionally, clarification is requested on whether SE3 alignment with ground truth trajectories is performed.
19. Could you elaborate on the yaw augmentation procedure used for TLIO and RoNIN?
20. It is surprising that despite requiring 10 times more FLOPs than the non-equivariant architectures, the runtime increase is less than 1 ms. Since there is no code release, can you comment more on the reasons for this efficiency?
21. In Figure 3b, it is not clear what 'rot. sense' means. Please explain how the frame is constructed from the network outputs with Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization for clarity.
22. There is a typo in the conclusion: 'respects eliminates' should be corrected.
23. It would be helpful to indicate that '*' means no-EKF in the captions of Tables 2 and 3, or simply remove the '*' since it is not applicable to RoNIN.
24. The accuracy improvements in terms of ATE and RTE metrics seem marginal compared to the increased complexity and runtime of the proposed method. On-device runtime is expected to be even higher. Nevertheless, the work is an interesting and novel application of learned canonicalization with potential for future work.
llm_feedback:
0. The paper should clearly and consistently define the main equivariance claim, distinguishing between subequivariance, SO(2)/O(2) equivariance, and strict O(3) equivariance, and explicitly state the assumptions under which strict equivariance holds.
1. The paper should address the identifiability and continuity of the canonical frame on the gravity-orthogonal plane, especially considering symmetries and degenerate motions that can cause discontinuities and frame flips.
2. The reflection handling method involving \(\omega = v_1 \times v_2\) preprocessing may introduce numerical issues and ambiguity that need to be addressed and analyzed.
3. The covariance modeling section has notation inconsistencies and lacks formal evaluation of calibration and filter consistency.
4. The metric definitions in the appendix are inconsistent and unclear, and the alignment protocols and units used in tables need to be explicitly stated and corrected.
5. The experimental comparisons lack fairness and clarity due to differences in training data fractions, missing baselines, unclear statistical significance, and confusing runtime reporting.
6. The architecture description lacks important implementation details necessary for reproducibility, such as activation functions, pooling types, normalization, orthonormalization methods, and contains typos and duplicated equations.
7. The theoretical presentation contains small errors and could be tightened by correctly defining groups, distinguishing actions on different vector spaces, and moving representation theory details to the appendix.
8. Additional targeted ablation studies are needed to better isolate the contributions of model capacity, canonicalization, equivariant layers, and gravity misalignment effects.
9. Writing and structure can be improved by reducing repetitive claims, consolidating similar figure captions, ensuring consistent dataset naming, and adding a limitations note about reflection equivariance and EKF compatibility.
10. Adding a practical plug-and-play recipe and releasing code or pseudocode for canonicalization and covariance unprojection would increase the paper's impact and adoption.
11. The three most important fixes are: fixing metric definitions and alignment protocols to ensure table consistency; making canonical frame construction explicit including orthonormalization and tie-breaking with degeneracy discussion; and cleaning up covariance transformation notation with stronger calibration and consistency evaluation.

title: Evading Data Contamination Detection for Language Models is (too) Easy
abstract: The benchmark performance of large language models (LLMs) has a high impact on their popularity and is thus of great importance to many model providers. However, the reliability of such benchmark scores as a measure of model quality gets compromised if the model is contaminated with benchmark data. While recent contamination detection methods try to address this issue, they overlook the possibility of deliberate contamination by malicious model providers aiming to evade detection. We propose a categorization of model providers based on their (de)contamination practices and argue that malicious contamination is of crucial importance as it casts doubt on the reliability of public benchmarks. To study this issue more rigorously, we analyze current contamination detection methods based on their assumptions. This analysis reveals a significant vulnerability in existing approaches: they do not account for rephrased benchmark data used during training by malicious actors. We demonstrate how exploiting this gap can result in significantly inflated benchmark scores while completely evading current detection methods.
human_feedback_list:
0. The paper's settings are unclear regarding contamination: the threat model's knowledge, capabilities, and concrete examples in text-based attacks are not defined; the attacker's influence on benchmarks, the nature of adversarial samples, and adversarial objectives are not explained; and the defense settings lack clarity about the detector/defender's role and possible actions to eliminate poisoning.
1. The paper uses overly complicated and non-standard terminology, which creates barriers to understanding. For example, 'contamination' is typically called 'poisoning'; 'actor' is usually 'attacker' or 'adversary'; 'de-contamination' is rarely used and 'mitigation' is preferred; 'verbatim' could be replaced by 'word-wise'; 'sample-level' could be more accurately expressed as 'sentence-level' or 'token-level'; and 'Problematic Data Contamination' in Definition 3 is inaccurately used since it discusses the possibility of injecting poisoning data D'.
2. The paper does not clearly explain why pre-training requires benchmarking data; the author probably means 'public' or 'crowdsourced' data instead.
3. The paper over-claims its contribution by stating 'We define four (de-)contamination settings...' when these settings are actually summarized from prior work rather than newly defined in this paper.
4. The role of model providers is unclear because in Section 3 they are described as both attackers and defenders. The paper should distinguish model providers from adversaries (the 'actor').
5. The paper has poor structure and incomplete content: it lacks a related work section; does not detail the proposed rephrasing attack such as prompts or techniques used; Definition 4 repeats Definitions 1 and 2; Definition 5 should be rigorously formulated and linked to technical design; and Figure 1 should introduce 'TPR' (true positive rate) and 'FPR' (false positive rate).
6. The contribution is not significant because the author simply uses 'rephrasing' without strong intuition or motivation for its usefulness.
7. The experiments are simple and have problematic settings: the author mentions several detection strategies but does not provide corresponding experiments; the paper fails to clarify how evasiveness is guaranteed or measured; no baseline attacks are included; and the trade-off between attack effectiveness and evasiveness is not discussed.
8. Many sentences in the paper seem to be generated by GPT. While using GPT for polishing is acceptable, the original writing may be logically inconsistent and unclear, and GPT alone cannot improve the logic.
9. The paper's Definition 3 is unclear and requires clarification to improve understanding.
10. The distinction between openly malicious and evasively malicious actors is problematic because openly malicious contamination is easily detected and removed by existing methods, making it hard to find realistic use cases for openly malicious attackers.
11. Sections 4 and 5 contain some repetition and unnecessary content, and would benefit from streamlining to improve clarity and conciseness.
12. The paper lacks detailed descriptions of the processes for data rephrasing and contamination insertion, especially regarding how rephrased data is generated, despite stating that code is provided.
13. The authors overclaim their contribution by failing to consider the technical feasibility and applicability of updating baseline data in real-world applications, resulting in attack methods that are more suitable for closed environments and may not work in open and real-time updating environments.
14. Does the EAL technique circumvent all detection methods mentioned in 'A Taxonomy for Data Contamination in Large Language Models,' including those that do not require pre-training data?
15. The paper demonstrates the vulnerability of current data contamination detection methods to rephrasing attacks but does not explore potential defenses against these attacks. Without suggestions for addressing this vulnerability, the work may feel incomplete.
16. Could you provide a more detailed breakdown of the performance of the attack across different benchmarks to clarify if the performance gain is consistent?
17. Would it be feasible to increase the sample size of the contaminated dataset to reduce variance and enhance the stability of the results?
18. The paper lacks novelty and technical depth, and its presentation is hard to follow. Specifically, the proposed attack strategy is not sufficiently detailed or clearly explained, making it difficult for readers to understand its design. The experimental section focuses mainly on evaluating existing methods and does not clearly demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed attack strategy. Additionally, the paper's organization is disjointed and lacks a coherent key idea, with unrelated sections placed consecutively, such as a discussion section that resembles a related work section.
llm_feedback:
0. Explicitly distinguish between inadvertent contamination (web scrape overlap), benchmark-targeted supervised finetuning (malicious setting), and semantic contamination (paraphrase/translation variants) to clarify the threat model and prevent misunderstanding that the attack is merely test-set training.
1. Make the attacker’s goals and constraints explicit, including the goal to inflate public benchmark scores without detection, constraints such as limited budget and auditor access, and capabilities like generating paraphrases with a strong LLM and filtering paraphrases using a surrogate detector.
2. Clarify the realism of the finetuning-only attacker by specifying which model class is targeted (base, instruction-tuned, etc.) and why finetuning is the realistic attack vector in those settings.
3. More directly contrast your work with Yang et al. (2023) by clearly highlighting your unique contributions such as evasion under a malicious actor model, evaluation against a suite of detectors including DPCC/CleanEval, and systematic actor taxonomy.
4. Avoid making overly broad claims such as “all current detection methods can be evaded” without formal justification; instead, narrow the claim to evaluated methods or those relying on verbatim signals, or provide a formal reduction argument.
5. Report per-benchmark results prominently rather than only averages, as behavior varies significantly across benchmarks.
6. Control for the effect of added compute or data versus contamination by including controls such as replacing contaminated samples with non-benchmark samples of similar format and adding a random paraphrase control where answers are mismatched.
7. Verify semantic equivalence of paraphrased samples systematically using automatic and human checks, such as recomputing answers for GSM8K and ensuring correct options remain correct for MCQ, and report estimated label error rates.
8. Reconsider omitting wrong options in MCQ contamination, as training only on question and correct answer without distractors weakens the contamination realism and detection evaluation; include conditions with full options paraphrased or clearly frame this as an attacker choice with discussion of implications.
9. Fix or justify the arbitrary MMLU subset selection by using random stratified domain sampling with a fixed seed or standard subsets from prior work, and report exact example counts per domain and split.
10. Use a more standard and robust evaluation methodology for MCQ accuracy instead of selecting options by highest ROUGE-L overlap, such as constrained decoding to output option labels or log-likelihood scoring over options.
11. Provide more detail and justification for the TruthfulQA scoring method used, mapping it to known evaluation protocols and reporting which subset/version is evaluated.
12. Calibrate and explicitly report the random guessing baseline for TPR@5%FPR to accurately contextualize detection performance.
13. Add stronger semantic similarity detectors as baselines, such as embedding-based retrieval methods (e.g., SBERT, OpenAI embeddings), to anchor the evaluation of semantic detectors and clarify the boundary of detection capabilities.

title: Learning Continually by Spectral Regularization
abstract: Loss of plasticity is a phenomenon where neural networks can become more difficult to train over the course of learning. Continual learning algorithms seek to mitigate this effect by sustaining good performance while maintaining network trainability. We develop a new technique for improving continual learning inspired by the observation that the singular values of the neural network parameters at initialization are an important factor for trainability during early phases of learning. From this perspective, we derive a new spectral regularizer for continual learning that better sustains these beneficial initialization properties throughout training. In particular, the regularizer keeps the maximum singular value of each layer close to one. Spectral regularization directly ensures that gradient diversity is maintained throughout training, which promotes continual trainability, while minimally interfering with performance in a single task. We present an experimental analysis that shows how the proposed spectral regularizer can sustain trainability and performance across a range of model architectures in continual supervised and reinforcement learning settings. Spectral regularization is less sensitive to hyperparameters while demonstrating better training in individual tasks, sustaining trainability as new tasks arrive, and achieving better generalization performance.

title: Understanding Heterophily for Graph Neural Networks
abstract: Graphs with heterophily have been regarded as challenging scenarios for Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), where nodes are connected with dissimilar neighbors through various patterns. In this paper, we present theoretical understandings of the impacts of different heterophily patterns for GNNs by incorporating the graph convolution (GC) operations into fully connected networks via the proposed Heterophilous Stochastic Block Models (HSBM), a general random graph model that can accommodate diverse heterophily patterns.  Firstly, we show that by applying a GC operation, the separability gains are determined by two factors, i.e., the Euclidean distance of the neighborhood distributions and $\sqrt{\mathbb{E}\left[\operatorname{deg}\right]}$, where $\mathbb{E}\left[\operatorname{deg}\right]$ is the averaged node degree. It reveals that the impact of heterophily on classification needs to be evaluated alongside the averaged node degree. Secondly, we show that the topological noise has a detrimental impact on separability, which is equivalent to degrading $\mathbb{E}\left[\operatorname{deg}\right]$. Finally, when applying multiple GC operations, we show that the separability gains are determined by the normalized distance of the $l$-powered neighborhood distributions. It indicates that the nodes still possess separability as $l$ goes to infinity in a wide range of regimes. Extensive experiments on both synthetic and real-world data verify the effectiveness of our theory.

title: Flag Aggregator: Scalable Distributed Training under Failures and Augmented Losses using Convex Optimization
abstract: Modern ML applications increasingly rely on complex deep learning models and large datasets. There has been an exponential growth in the amount of computation needed to train the largest models. Therefore, to scale computation and data, these models are inevitably trained in a distributed manner in clusters of nodes, and their updates are aggregated before being applied to the model. However, a distributed setup is prone to Byzantine failures of individual nodes, components, and software. With data augmentation added to these settings, there is a critical need for robust and efficient aggregation systems. We define the quality of workers as reconstruction ratios $\in (0,1]$, and formulate aggregation as a Maximum Likelihood Estimation procedure using Beta densities. We show that the Regularized form of log-likelihood wrt subspace can be approximately solved using iterative least squares solver, and provide convergence guarantees using recent Convex Optimization landscape results. Our empirical findings demonstrate that our approach significantly enhances the robustness of state-of-the-art Byzantine resilient aggregators. We evaluate our method in a distributed setup with a parameter server, and show simultaneous improvements in communication efficiency and accuracy across various tasks. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/hamidralmasi/FlagAggregator

title: Score Forgetting Distillation: A Swift, Data-Free Method for Machine Unlearning in Diffusion Models
abstract: The machine learning community is increasingly recognizing the importance of fostering trust and safety in modern generative AI (GenAI) models. We posit machine unlearning (MU) as a crucial foundation for developing safe, secure, and trustworthy GenAI models. Traditional MU methods often rely on stringent assumptions and require access to real data. This paper introduces Score Forgetting Distillation (SFD), an innovative MU approach that promotes the forgetting of undesirable information in diffusion models by aligning the conditional scores of "unsafe" classes or concepts with those of "safe" ones. To eliminate the need for real data, our SFD framework incorporates a score-based MU loss into the score distillation objective of a pretrained diffusion model. This serves as a regularization term that preserves desired generation capabilities while enabling the production of synthetic data through a one-step generator. Our experiments on pretrained label-conditional and text-to-image diffusion models demonstrate that our method effectively accelerates the forgetting of target classes or concepts during generation, while preserving the quality of other classes or concepts. This unlearned and distilled diffusion not only pioneers a novel concept in MU but also accelerates the generation speed of diffusion models. Our experiments and studies on a range of diffusion models and datasets confirm that our approach is generalizable, effective, and advantageous for MU in diffusion models. Code is available at https://github.com/tqch/score-forgetting-distillation. ($\textbf{Warning:}$ This paper contains sexually explicit imagery, discussions of pornography, racially-charged terminology, and other content that some readers may find disturbing, distressing, and/or offensive.)
